schools.summary: Mark's perfect 'come with power' (vs Matthew 16:28); Lightfoot reads the destruction of Jerusalem; the second coming did not happen in that generation (Robertson).
Pseudo-Jerome and most fathers refer it to the Transfiguration that follows — the glory shown that they might not fear the cross.
Henry: the Messiah's kingdom set up by the fall of the Jewish polity and conquest of idolatry.
Wesley: it began at Pentecost, when three thousand were converted at once (Acts 2:41).

debates.question: What is the 'kingdom come with power' that some would live to see?

sides.position: Pentecost and the spread of the gospel

outliers.note: A catechetical passage on the ways of gaining eternal life, tangential to the verse.

schools.summary: The three taken 'by themselves' (only in Mark); Luke adds 'to pray' (Luke 9:28); the mountain perhaps near Caesarea Philippi, not Tabor (Lightfoot, Clarke).
Wesley links 'transfigured' to the form of God and form of a servant (Philippians 2:6-7).
The dazzling whiteness no fuller could rival, Mark's homely vivid touch; snow-capped Hermon perhaps visible; 'as snow' wanting in the best MSS; 'fuller' = scourer (Barnes).
Moses and Elijah, heads of Law and Prophets; both prophets with mysterious deaths (Robertson); 'Elijah whom they expected, Moses whom they did not' (Wesley).
The Cyril of Jerusalem entry is on Pentecost, tangential to the verse.

outliers.note: A passage on the descent of the Spirit at Pentecost, not on the verse.

schools.summary: Mark gives the Hebrew 'Rabbi' for 'Master' (eyewitness touch); the joy of the holy mount (Psalm 84:10); Peter as the principal speaker.
Peter spoke 'not knowing what he said', perhaps embarrassed at having slept (Luke 9:32); the disciples had not heard the talk of Christ's 'decease'; sore afraid.
Mark omits 'in whom I am well pleased' (Matthew 17:5); 'hear him' fulfils the prophet like Moses (Deuteronomy 18:15); the cloud overshadowing all.
Wesley: the cloud of glory that left at Moses' death returns to honour Christ, the great Prophet — hear him preferably to Moses and Elijah.
Cyril of Jerusalem turns the verse to a polemic urging worship of the Son the Father owns.
Mark's graphic 'sudden glance around' (vs Matthew 17:8) when the cloud was gone; they saw Jesus only, and were sore afraid.
Chrysostom and Theophylact: the silence lest men be offended hearing such glory of one about to be crucified; the Passion implies the cause.
Mark's 'save when' he should rise; a high and holy secret for the three (Robertson).
Pseudo-Jerome reads the Markan clause that when death is swallowed in victory we shall have no memory for former things.

outliers.note: A passage on the great tribulation, tangential to the verse.

schools.summary: They kept the charge of silence but debated what rising from the dead meant (Mark 8:31); a characteristic Markan analysis of their perplexity; the clause peculiar to Mark.
The disciples' question prompted by seeing Elijah; the scribes' teaching from Malachi 4:5 that Elijah must come first (Matthew 17:10-13).
'Restoreth all things' = the Baptist as the promised Elijah (Malachi 4:5-6); Elijah's coming not inconsistent with the Son of man's suffering (Isaiah 53:3).
Gill: Christ allowed the scribes' point was right when rightly understood.
The Cyril of Jerusalem entry is a Passion-meditation, tangential to the verse.
Elijah is come in John the Baptist (Matthew 11:14; Luke 1:17, 'spirit and power of Elias'); 'as it is written of him' (peculiar to Mark) points to Malachi and the parallel careers.
The 'scribes questioning' (only in Mark) gleefully nagging the failed nine; Mark's fuller account; the scribes confounding the disciples in Christ's absence.
Theophylact: the Pharisees seized the hour of Christ's absence; Bede contrasts the devotionless scribes with the amazed people; Pseudo-Jerome: envy slays the little ones.
Calvin and Henry: Christ came seasonably when the disciples were aground and the scribes had gained an advantage.
The crowd's amazement (peculiar to Mark) at his sudden, opportune appearance; Ellicott and Wesley wonder at a lingering radiance from the Transfiguration.
Christ noticed the embarrassment of the nine and at once took hold of the situation (Robertson); the question put to the scribes pressing the disciples (Gill).
The father's direct, pathetic account; the 'dumb spirit' (peculiar to Mark) depriving the boy of speech (Matthew 17:14).
Calvin: Matthew's 'lunatic' and Mark's 'dumb spirit' agree — possession mixed with natural epilepsy affected by the moon.
The graphic verbs — seizes down, convulses, foams, grinds the teeth, withers (cf. James 1:11); the disciples lacked the strength; gnashing and pining peculiar to Mark.
The disciples' unbelief led to the fiasco; 'faithless' = unbelieving (Deuteronomy 32:20); we can come to Jesus when others fail (Robertson); the rebuke includes the father (Gill).
The violent convulsion at Christ's presence, 'wallowing foaming' (found only in Mark); the demon raging, knowing his time short (Clarke).
Calvin: the nearer Christ's grace, the fiercer Satan's fury; his presence awakens the enemy like a trumpet — be not dismayed.
The question sets the chronic, seemingly hopeless case in relief (the fire and waters peculiar to Mark), that the cure might be more remarkable.
Calvin: the affliction not for personal sin but a secret judgment of God to try obedience (cf. John 9:3).
'If thou canst' suggests doubt born of the disciples' failure; the father makes the boy's case his own, 'help us now' (Robertson); the accents of despair (Ellicott).
Calvin: he renders Christ little honour, supposing his power limited; faith must embrace the boundless power of God.
Christ throws the 'if' back: 'all things can to the one who believes' (Robertson); God does nothing without faith (Barnes); best MSS omit 'believe', reading sad surprise (Ellicott).
Calvin: not the Lord but the narrowness of our faith makes his benefits come in drops; unbelief shuts the heart.

themes: 'all things are possible to him that believeth'

schools.summary: An exact description of his state — faith craving more; 'help' the present imperative of continuous aid; 'with tears' wanting in the best MSS.
Calvin: he believes yet confesses unbelief — faith never perfect, we are partly unbelievers, yet reckoned believers for a small portion of faith.
Cyril of Jerusalem (on point): Lazarus raised by his sisters' faith supplying the dead man's lack.

outliers.note: The note here is misplaced, not bearing on the verse.

schools.summary: The emphatic 'I charge thee' where the disciples failed; Christ addresses the demon as a real personal cause (against mere-disease readings); 'enter no more' a tender adaptation to the father's fear of relapse.
The boy as one dead from the spasm, the demon doing all possible harm in leaving; the rending, corpse-like fall, and cry of 'the many' peculiar to Mark.
Wesley: the body sometimes suffers when God delivers the soul from Satan.
Barnes: the upholding hand of God (Isaiah 41:13) lifts the boy up.
Gill: Christ took hold of the child's hand and raised him.
Indoors the nine seek an explanation for their failure though they had cast out demons before (Mark 2:16 for the interrogative); withdrawn for retirement (Gill).
'And fasting' wanting in the best MSS, a late ascetic addition (cf. 1 Corinthians 7:5); the nine were powerless because prayerless and self-complacent, with little faith (Matthew 17:20).
Clarke reads the demon as deep-rooted vice overcome only through extraordinary humiliation.

debates.question: Is 'and fasting' part of the original text?

sides.position: No — wanting in the best MSS, a late ascetic addition

themes: powers that yield only to prayer

schools.summary: He sought privacy, no longer the hero of Galilee (Mark 7:24), to instruct the Twelve fully on his sufferings (Matthew 17:22).
Theophylact: the Passion taught after miracles, lest he seem to suffer from weakness, with the resurrection added; Bede: he mingles sorrow and joy to steady their minds.
Henry: he passed through Galilee privately as the time of his sufferings drew nigh, his heart set on his suffering-work.
The imperfect 'he taught' explains the secrecy — renewing the prediction (Mark 8:31), his rising 'after three days'; 'delivered' to make atonement (Barnes).
Cyril of Jerusalem (on point): he came to his passion willingly, lest the world perish in its sins.
They continued not to understand, afraid to ask, perhaps remembering the 'Satan' rebuke (Mark 8:33); the analysis in Mark and Luke, not Matthew.
Wesley: they could not reconcile his death with their notions of a temporal kingdom.
They disputed about relative rank, not his death; Jesus suspected and pushed the matter deeper (Matthew 18:1; Luke 9:46); in the house, perhaps Peter's.
They held their peace, ashamed the Master had discovered their jealous rivalry — a canker in their hearts, not an abstract query (Robertson).
Wesley: the dispute was over who should be prime minister in his kingdom.
He sat down as teacher and gave the rule of greatness — the first must be last of all and servant of all (Mark 10:43; Matthew 20:26).

themes: last of all, servant of all

schools.summary: The participle 'taken him in his arms' (only here and Mark 10:16), perhaps folding the arms round him; the child set in the midst as an object lesson.

outliers.note: The note here is misplaced, not bearing on the verse.

schools.summary: The 'child' = the humblest believer like a child (Gill); an object lesson to the Twelve's conceit; receiving the child for Christ's sake reaches up to the Father (John 14:23).
The man perhaps a disciple of John or of the seventy (Luke 10:1), not a mere exorcist; John expected praise but showed narrow prejudice (Robertson).
Bede: drive no one from partial goodness but stir him to more; the Lord extends his name even by the unworthy; Augustine harmonises with Luke 11:23.
Calvin and Wesley draw the warning against bigotry and sectarian narrowness.
'Stop hindering him' (present imperative); a true miracle in his name proves the doer no quick enemy; Moses on Eldad and Medad (Numbers 11:29), Paul's joy (Philippians 1:18).
Calvin: bear with the irregular and rejoice when God is glorified; Clarke: bigotry has no countenance here.
The complement of Matthew 12:30 — both needed; one not against you, doing the same work, is on your side; Wesley: rigorous in judging self, candid in judging others.
Calvin: not a license for rash men to disturb order, but those who do no harm are profitable; raise no quarrel till constrained.

debates.question: How does this square with 'he that is not with me is against me' (Matthew 12:30)?

sides.position: Both are needed — no neutrality toward Satan, charity toward fellow-labourers

themes: 'not against us is on our part'

schools.summary: The cup of water given 'in his name' from love to Christ, surely rewarded; 'because ye are Christ's' the bond of universal brotherhood (Romans 8:9); reiteration of Matthew 10:42.
Henry: the honour of belonging to Christ; relieving his poor turns a good account.
The Cyril of Jerusalem note here is on eternal life, tangential.
The 'little ones' are not infants but the humblest believers (Gill); to offend them merits worse than drowning with a millstone (Matthew 18:6).

outliers.note: The note here concerns the parable of the sower, not this verse.

schools.summary: 'Hell' = Gehenna (Valley of Hinnom), the unquenchable fire (Matthew 18:8); textual doubt over the repeated clause; Lightfoot's rabbinic parallel.
Bede, Chrysostom, the Gloss: the 'hand' is an intimate but harmful friend to be cut off — better life without him than hell with him; the worm and fire as remorse.
Wesley: whatever, person or thing, dear as a hand yet hindering us in God's ways, renounce it.

debates.question: What does 'cut off the hand' refer to?

sides.position: Besetting sins and members that lead to sin

schools.summary: The image from Isaiah 66:24 (undying worm, unquenched fire); the oldest MSS omit vv. 44 and 46 as a repetition of v. 48 (Robertson).
Gill: the worm is the gnawing conscience, the fire God's wrath, both perpetual.
Wesley: the worm the soul's own undying remorse, the fire material or worse, unquenched for ever.
Gill reads the 'foot' as a support or connection — one on whom a man leans for subsistence — to be shunned if it leads him into sin or away from Christ.
Robertson: the clause, like v. 44, omitted by the oldest MSS as a repetition of v. 48; numbering retained.
Gill: the figures design the eternity of hell torments — 'from thence there can be no deliverance'.
Mark's 'kingdom of God' for Matthew's 'life' (Matthew 18:9); the 'one-eyed' vernacular; the eye as the dearest member to be plucked out if a snare (Gill).
The Cyril of Jerusalem entry treats baptism, tangential to the verse.

outliers.note: A passage on baptism by water and the Spirit, not on the verse.

schools.summary: The genuine, climactic refrain (unlike vv. 44, 46), combining the gnawing worm and burning flame from Isaiah 66:24 — no figure equals the reality.
Gill: repeated to fix an awful impression; the Persic, 'from whence thou shalt never find redemption'.

outliers.note: The note here concerns the lamp, not this verse.

schools.summary: A famously difficult saying: fire preserving the wicked in torment, or the apostles prepared as a salted sacrifice (Leviticus 2:13; Isaiah 66:24); textual difficulties noted.
Calvin: the reprobate salted with eternal fire; believers to offer themselves now, seasoned as devoted sacrifices; Wesley likewise.

sides.position: The fire of hell preserves the wicked in torment
Believers seasoned by trial as a sacrifice (Leviticus 2:13)

schools.summary: They are the salt of the earth (Matthew 5:13); the salt is the inward grace of self-denial; 'have peace' points back to the contention (v. 33), purity from selfish aims preserving peace.
Calvin: the salt is the seasoning of grace and the Spirit's wisdom (Colossians 4:6), promoting mutual peace; Wesley: retain your savour and have peace.
